skill.name: zoom-out
skill.description: Tell the agent to zoom out and give broader context or a higher-level perspective. Use when you're unfamiliar with a section of code or need to understand how it fits into the bigger picture.
skill.summary: Get a higher-level overview of unfamiliar code with module maps and domain vocabulary
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md:3 for 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' but this is a false positive. Line 3 is a YAML description field containing the word 'description' which triggered a pattern match on 'crypt'. No cryptographic algorithms exist in this skill. The skill is a simple meta-instruction requesting broader context from an AI agent. No malicious patterns detected. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Zoom Out and See the Big Picture
content.value_statement: Navigating unfamiliar codebases is disorienting and time-consuming. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code provide a structured overview of code architecture using your project's domain vocabulary, reducing context-switching time.
actual_capabilities: Requests an AI agent to rise above detailed implementation and view code at a higher abstraction layer
Generates a map of relevant modules and their relationships
Uses project-specific domain vocabulary to describe components
Identifies callers and dependencies for a given code section
Provides orientation when entering unfamiliar parts of a codebase
limitations: Does not execute code or run commands - purely an instructional prompt
Quality of response depends on the AI agent's knowledge of your codebase
Cannot discover information the AI agent does not already have access to
Does not modify or write code - only requests analysis and explanation

use_cases.title: Onboarding to a New Codebase Module
use_cases.description: Junior developers joining a team use this skill to quickly orient themselves when first exploring unfamiliar feature areas.

use_cases.title: Debugging Across Module Boundaries
use_cases.description: Senior engineers trace issues through unknown subsystems by requesting architectural context before diving into specific files.

use_cases.title: Code Review Context
use_cases.description: Team members reviewing pull requests for unfamiliar areas use this to understand how changes fit into the broader system.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner - Basic Request
prompt_templates.prompt: I don't know this area of code well. Go up a layer of abstraction. Give me a map of all the relevant modules and callers.

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate - With Domain Context
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm new to the authentication module. Zoom out and explain how it connects to the user service and session management using our domain terms.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced - Focused Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Before I dive into this legacy code, zoom out and give me the high-level architecture, key entry points, and which domain concepts map to which files.

prompt_templates.title: Expert - Targeted Dependency Map
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm going to refactor the payment processor. Zoom out and map out all the modules that call into it, what it depends on, and what domain concepts it implements.

output_examples.input: I don't know this area of code well. Go up a layer of abstraction.
output_examples.output: Here's the architecture overview: The codebase follows a layered architecture. This area is in the service layer. Key modules: [UserService - handles user lifecycle], [AuthModule - manages authentication tokens], [ProfileRepository - handles data persistence]. Callers: API routes → this service → external APIs. Key domain concepts: 'Tenant' (organization), 'Credential' (auth token), 'Session' (user context).

output_examples.input: Zoom out on the payment processing module for me.
output_examples.output: Payment Processing Architecture: This module sits at the boundary between the Order domain and external payment providers. It orchestrates: PaymentGateway (external Stripe integration), TransactionLog (audit trail), RefundHandler (reversal logic). Entry points: createPayment(), capturePayment(), refundPayment(). It raises domain events: PaymentAuthorized, PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed.

best_practices: Use this skill before diving into unfamiliar code to reduce time spent orienting
Combine with specific file references for more targeted overviews
Use project domain terms in your follow-up questions to get more accurate mappings
anti_patterns: Do not use repeatedly in the same session - it interrupts flow once you have the overview
Do not expect runtime data or current database state - this shows static architecture
Do not use when you already know the domain - it provides generic overviews that may not add value

faq.question: How is this different from just asking 'what does this code do'?
faq.answer: Zoom-out specifically requests architectural context and module relationships, not just line-by-line explanations. It asks for a map using domain vocabulary.

faq.question: Can this help me understand a completely new codebase from scratch?
faq.answer: Partially. The AI agent can only explain code it has access to. For completely new codebases, start with README files and entry points first.

faq.question: Will this show me runtime behavior or dynamic data?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides static architectural overview. It cannot query databases, show current state, or demonstrate runtime behavior.

faq.question: Is this useful for monorepos with many packages?
faq.answer: Yes. It works well for understanding how packages relate to each other and which domain modules are involved in cross-package dependencies.

faq.question: Can I use this during pair programming sessions?
faq.answer: Absolutely. Both the driver and navigator can use this to establish shared understanding of unfamiliar areas during pairing.

faq.question: How often should I use this skill in a session?
faq.answer: Use it once per new area you explore. After you have the overview, continue with focused questions. Repeated zoom-out requests break concentration.
